label: Ineffective Customer Support
definition: Users report unhelpful, unresponsive or inaccessible customer service: unresolved complaints, refusal to take responsibility, and unfulfilled refund promises.
merge_reason: Definitions are near-identical — both describe unhelpful, unaccountable support. No coder could split them reliably.

label: Order and Delivery Failures
definition: The order did not arrive as promised: items incorrect, missing, damaged or short in quantity, wrong location, or poor handling by delivery personnel.
merge_reason: All three span 'what arrived was wrong' and 'how it arrived was wrong'. delivery_issues explicitly covers both, so the boundary between the three is not codeable.

label: Pricing and Fees
definition: Prices higher than market or other platforms, plus delivery charges, handling fees, surge pricing and minimum cart limits.
merge_reason: Both cover above-market prices AND added fees; the labels differ but the definitions overlap almost entirely.
relevance_reason: Cost of the basket, not the cost of a first try.

label: Payment, Cancellation and Refunds
definition: Payment failures, duplicate orders, platform-side cancellations, cancellation fees, and refunds that are delayed or never arrive.
merge_reason: Both centre on money not moving correctly — cancellations and refunds appear in each definition.

label: Product Quality Concerns
definition: Users express dissatisfaction with the quality of perishable items (e.g., curdled milk), lack of visible expiry dates, or receiving items that do not match product descriptions (e.g., warm drinks, wrong umbrella).
relevance_reason: Definition is about perishables arriving spoiled and items not matching description — an operational failure, not hesitancy about an unfamiliar category. Previously flagged core; this is the correction.

label: Frequent Out-of-Stock Items
definition: Essential items are often unavailable or marked 'out of stock,' defeating the purpose of a quick delivery service.
relevance_reason: Concerns essentials being out of stock, not the discoverability of new categories. Previously core.

label: Value of Convenience and Speed
definition: Users appreciate the quick delivery and convenience of ordering essentials from home, saving time and effort.
relevance_reason: Appreciation of speed. Satisfaction with the service, with no exploration mechanism in the definition. NOT merged with convenience_as_primary_driver for exactly this reason.

label: Breaches of Privacy and Discretion
definition: Users report concerns about delivery personnel knowing their order contents, leading to uncomfortable or inappropriate interactions, especially with sensitive items.
relevance_reason: Delivery-personnel discretion. No connection to category exploration. Previously core.

label: Delivery Time Expectations
definition: Discussions around the 10-minute delivery promise, its impact on workers, and actual delivery times.
relevance_reason: Debate about the 10-minute promise and its effect on workers.

label: App Technical Issues
definition: Users encounter glitches with the app, such as location unavailability, shopping cart emptying, or misleading UX regarding offers and charges.

label: Habitual Purchases: Value for Money
definition: Users stick to certain products or categories (e.g., protein shakes) based on cost-effectiveness and perceived value, often comparing prices across brands or platforms.
relevance_reason: Explicitly about sticking to known products and categories.

label: Lack of Category Exploration Deterrents
definition: Users are discouraged from trying new categories due to negative experiences with product quality, misleading listings, or the hassle of returns for unfamiliar items.
relevance_reason: Directly about being discouraged from trying new categories.

label: Convenience as Primary Driver
definition: Users primarily use quick-commerce for speed and convenience, especially for daily essentials or sudden requirements, which reinforces buying familiar items rather than exploring new ones.
relevance_reason: Definition states convenience 'reinforces buying familiar items rather than exploring new ones' — a named mechanism for not exploring.
